narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there with your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and LSAT score of 32. Your background, including your lower-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, reflects the real challenges many face on this path. The admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated an 88.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam, leading to the tough decision to reject your application right now. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school—your Year 3 decile of 1 was the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting failure, as it signaled lower performance relative to peers, where the typical student sits around the middle of the pack. Your Year 1 decile of 1 reinforced this concern, ranking as the second strongest influence in the same direction, well below the average fifth or sixth decile. Your LSAT score also contributed toward the failure prediction, since 32 falls noticeably short of the dataset average of about 37 that successful students typically have. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, as your 3.10 is close to the average of 3.21 and showed some solid college work. Being a full-time student helped a little too, aligning with the vast majority of passers who chose that route. Overall, compared to typical students who pass—those with mid-range deciles, higher LSATs, and similar GPAs—your early academic standings stood out as the key gaps tipping the scales. While this stings, especially given the barriers Black women often navigate in these spaces, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting those decile rankings through targeted academic improvement or retaking the LSAT for a higher score. We encourage you to seek mentorship, prep courses, or community resources tailored for students like you—your resilience can turn this around. Keep pushing forward; law needs voices like yours.
